description: A sulfonylurea used for type 2 diabetes; it lowers blood sugar by stimulating the pancreas to release insulin. Among the sulfonylureas it has the fastest onset and shortest duration, so the immediate-release tablet is taken shortly before meals.

halfLife.conditions: Elimination half-life 2–4 h in normal subjects, essentially the SAME whether given orally or intravenously (FDA Glucotrol immediate-release label) — evidence that first-pass metabolism is not significant (F ~100%). Short for a sulfonylurea, which is why glipizide is dosed before meals rather than once daily. Half-life gives ke; clearance is not stored.

vd.conditions: 'The mean apparent volume of distribution was approximately 10 liters' (FDA Glucotrol XL label; ~11.7 L in earlier single-dose PK work) — i.e. ~0.15 L/kg against the 70 kg reference subject. A SMALL volume — glipizide is ~98–99% plasma-protein bound and stays largely intravascular, like warfarin (~0.14 L/kg). Glipizide is technically two-compartment, but the distribution volume (~10 L) is essentially its steady-state volume, so the distribution phase is negligible and the oral profile reads cleanly as one-compartment (see notes).

F.conditions: 'Gastrointestinal absorption of glipizide in man is uniform, rapid, and essentially complete' with ~100% bioavailability and no significant first-pass loss (FDA Glucotrol label; the oral and IV half-lives coincide). Modelled as F = 1. There is no pre-systemic (firstPassFraction) term. Drives the oral parent curve (C ~ Dose/Vd).

tmax.conditions: 'Peak plasma concentrations occur 1 to 3 hours after a single oral dose' of the immediate-release tablet (FDA Glucotrol label); representative point value 1.5 h, from which ka is derived at derivation time. (The extended-release Glucotrol XL formulation, not modelled here, deliberately flattens this peak.)

fda_label.title: GLUCOTROL (glipizide) tablets — FDA Structured Product Label (DailyMed), Clinical Pharmacology: gastrointestinal absorption uniform, rapid and essentially complete (bioavailability ~100%); peak plasma concentration 1–3 h after a single oral dose; elimination half-life 2–4 h in normal subjects whether given intravenously or orally (first-pass metabolism not significant); ~98–99% bound to plasma proteins; the primary metabolites are inactive hydroxylation products and polar conjugates, <10% excreted unchanged in urine.

fda_label_xl.title: GLUCOTROL XL (glipizide extended-release) tablets — FDA Structured Product Label (DailyMed), Clinical Pharmacology: 'the pharmacokinetics of glipizide were linear over the dose range of 5 to 60 mg ... in that the plasma drug concentrations increased proportionately with dose'; 'the mean apparent volume of distribution was approximately 10 liters'; the major metabolites have no hypoglycaemic activity, though a minor metabolite is reported to have 1/10 to 1/3 the activity of the parent.

metabolism: Glipizide is almost completely broken down in the liver (mainly by the enzyme CYP2C9). Its major metabolites — hydroxylation products and polar conjugates — have no blood-sugar-lowering activity, so no metabolite line is drawn, and less than a tenth of the dose leaves the body unchanged in the urine. (One minor metabolite is reported to retain a small fraction — roughly a tenth to a third — of the parent's activity, but it is a minor pathway and is not modelled.) The important honesty point is that concentration is not effect: the plotted curve is the drug level, but glipizide works by prompting insulin release, and that insulin response — and the fall in blood glucose — is glucose-dependent and does not simply track the drug concentration (which is why the tablet is timed to a meal). This is an educational illustration, not clinical or dosing guidance.
notes:
The set's first SULFONYLUREA — a genuinely new therapeutic class (an insulin secretagogue), distinct from metformin's biguanide mechanism, so it widens the antidiabetic coverage rather than duplicating it. It is a clean, short-half-life, oral one-compartment drug with an unusually small volume of distribution. Both sources are FDA labels opened during curation (the immediate-release Glucotrol label for absorption/half-life/binding/metabolites, the Glucotrol XL label for the linearity statement and the volume of distribution).

WHY ONE COMPARTMENT (a clean collapse, unlike the drugs deferred this pass). Glipizide is technically two-compartment after an IV dose, but its distribution volume (~10 L, the XL label's apparent volume) is essentially its steady-state volume — the peripheral compartment is tiny — so the distribution phase is negligible and, given orally (Tmax 1–3 h masks even that brief phase), the profile reads cleanly as one-compartment. This is the SAME oral-collapse posture as procainamide/cefotaxime, but here it is barely an approximation (contrast escitalopram and sotalol, deferred this pass, whose central and steady-state volumes differ enough that a one-compartment collapse would misplace the peak or the exposure).

SMALL VOLUME, HIGH BINDING — BUT STILL LINEAR (the naproxen contrast, a teaching point). Glipizide's Vd ~0.15 L/kg (~10 L, barely above plasma volume) comes from its ~98–99% plasma-protein binding, the same small-volume/high-binding axis as warfarin (~0.14 L/kg). Yet glipizide is LINEAR while naproxen (also ~99% bound, deferred this pass for concentration-dependent binding) is not — because therapeutic glipizide plasma concentrations are in the ng/mL range, roughly a thousandfold below albumin binding saturation, so the bound fraction stays constant with dose. Naproxen's therapeutic concentrations are in the mg/L range, high enough to approach saturation and make its clearance dose-dependent. Same 99%-bound headline, opposite linearity verdict — because of WHERE on the binding curve the drug sits.

LINEARITY (sourced). The FDA Glucotrol XL label states directly that 'the pharmacokinetics of glipizide were linear over the dose range of 5 to 60 mg ... plasma drug concentrations increased proportionately with dose' — dose-proportional AUC/Cmax means first-order elimination and valid superposition (linear: true). (This is a disposition property of the drug substance, established with the extended-release formulation but formulation-independent; the modelled immediate-release curve inherits it.)

ROUTE. Oral only — glipizide is a mealtime tablet with no marketed IV product (the IV data exist only from PK research). The extended-release Glucotrol XL is a different release profile, not modelled here.

CONCENTRATION IS NOT EFFECT (a warfarin-style honesty caveat). The curve is plasma glipizide; the drug's action is to trigger insulin secretion, and the glucose-lowering effect is glucose-dependent and time-shifted relative to the concentration peak — the reason it is dosed to a meal. The plot says nothing about blood glucose or hypoglycaemia risk.

MAGNITUDE CHECK (built engine curve). A 5 mg oral dose (F 1.0, Vd ~10.5 L, ke = ln2/3 h, ka from Tmax 1.5 h) peaks near ~330 ng/mL, within the reported ~300–450 ng/mL range for 5 mg glipizide. Educational illustration only — not for dosing.
